name: Ériu
description: Ériu is a goddess in Irish mythology, considered one of the three sovereign goddesses of Ireland, along with her sisters Banba and Fódla. These goddesses were daughters of Ernmas of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Ériu is often seen as the personification of Ireland itself, her name being the source of the modern name 'Ireland' (Éire). She is associated with sovereignty, land, and the power of the land. In some accounts, she is the wife of Mac Grianna, one of the three kings who ruled Ireland before the arrival of the Gaels. When the Milesians (Gaels) arrived, Ériu met them and prophesied that they would rule the land, granting them permission to settle. Her acceptance of the Milesians is crucial to their legitimacy as rulers. She is depicted as a powerful and wise figure, deeply connected to the land and its destiny. Her role in the myths is vital in establishing the divine right of the Gaels to rule Ireland and establishing a connection between the land and its people. She represents the enduring spirit of Ireland and its connection to the divine feminine. Ériu's influence continues to be felt in Irish culture and identity, symbolizing the land's ancient heritage and enduring spirit.
subtitle: Sovereign Goddess of Ireland

keyMyths.title: Meeting the Milesians
keyMyths.description: Ériu meets the Milesians upon their arrival in Ireland and prophesies their rule, granting them permission to settle. This act legitimizes their claim to the land.
keyMyths.source: Lebor na hUidre (Book of the Dun Cow)

keyMyths.title: Sovereignty Goddess
keyMyths.description: Ériu, along with her sisters, represents the sovereignty of Ireland. Her acceptance is necessary for any king to rightfully rule the land.

keyMyths.title: Names of Ireland
keyMyths.description: Ireland has been known by many names, including Ériu, Banba, and Fódla, each representing a different aspect of the land's spirit and history.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: A compilation of early Irish myths and legends.
sources.text: A compilation of early Irish myths and legends.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary of Irish mythological figures and concepts.
sources.text: A comprehensive dictionary of Irish mythological figures and concepts.

sources.author: John Matthews
sources.description: Explores the history, mythology, and spirituality of the Celtic peoples.
sources.text: Explores the history, mythology, and spirituality of the Celtic peoples.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While direct worship practices are not extensively documented, it is believed that offerings related to agriculture and the land would have been made to Ériu to ensure fertility and prosperity. These offerings likely included crops, milk, and other agricultural products.

worshipPractices.name: Invocation
worshipPractices.description: Ériu, along with Banba and Fódla, was likely invoked in rituals and ceremonies related to kingship and sovereignty. The act of a king marrying the land, represented by Ériu, ensured his legitimacy and the prosperity of his reign.

festivals.name: Samhain
festivals.description: As a goddess connected to the land and sovereignty, Ériu may have been honored during Samhain, a festival marking the end of the harvest season and the beginning of winter. This was a time when the veil between worlds was thin, and connections to the deities of the land were strengthened.

festivals.name: Imbolc
festivals.description: Imbolc, a festival celebrating the coming of spring and the renewal of life, may have also been associated with Ériu. As a goddess of fertility and the land, her influence would have been felt as the earth began to awaken after winter.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ériu's legacy is deeply embedded in Irish national identity. Her name, Éire, is the Irish name for Ireland and appears on currency and official documents. She is a symbol of Irish sovereignty and the enduring spirit of the land, frequently referenced in literature, art, and political discourse as a personification of Ireland.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Ériu is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, belonging to the Tuatha Dé Danann, the people of the goddess Danu. She is described as one of the daughters of Ernmas, a significant figure whose offspring are important deities. Her sisters are Banba and Fódla, and together, these three are considered the sovereign goddesses of Ireland. Ériu's lineage connects her to the very roots of the divine race that shaped the land. The circumstances of her birth are not detailed in surviving texts, but her association with Ernmas places her firmly within the powerful and magical Tuatha Dé Danann. This divine heritage grants her a natural authority and connection to the land, setting the stage for her role as a sovereign goddess. Her name itself is of ancient origin, suggesting a deep connection to the land of Ireland, predating even the arrival of the Gaels.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most significant stories involving Ériu concerns the arrival of the Milesians, the ancestors of the Gaels, in Ireland. As they approached the shores, Ériu, along with her sisters Banba and Fódla, met them. Each sister asked that the island be named after her. Ériu prophesied that the Milesians would rule the land, and in return, the island would be named after her – Éire. This encounter highlights Ériu's role as a figure of sovereignty and prophecy. Another myth involves her marriage to Mac Gréine, one of the three sons of Cermad, who ruled Ireland in turn after the Tuatha Dé Danann. This marriage further solidifies her connection to the land and the ruling power. While specific tales of Ériu are limited, her presence is a constant throughout the mythological cycle, signifying the enduring spirit of Ireland.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct evidence of specific cult practices dedicated solely to Ériu is scarce, but her importance as a sovereign goddess suggests that she was honored through rituals and ceremonies connected to kingship and the land. The inauguration of kings likely involved symbolic acts that acknowledged the king's union with the land, personified by Ériu. Offerings related to agriculture and fertility would have been appropriate, seeking her blessing for a bountiful harvest and the prosperity of the people. It is plausible that sacred sites, such as hills and springs, were associated with Ériu, where people would have made offerings and prayers. Given the interconnectedness of the Celtic pantheon, Ériu's influence would have been woven into broader religious practices rather than existing as a separate, distinct cult.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ériu's most enduring legacy is undoubtedly her name, which has become synonymous with Ireland itself. The Irish word for Ireland is 'Éire,' directly derived from her name, and it is prominently featured on Irish currency, official documents, and in the national anthem. This connection ensures that Ériu's name and presence remain a constant reminder of Ireland's ancient heritage. She is often invoked as a symbol of Irish sovereignty and national identity, representing the land's spirit and resilience. In literature and art, Ériu is frequently portrayed as a beautiful woman, embodying the beauty and power of the Irish landscape. Her influence extends beyond mere symbolism, inspiring a deep sense of connection to the land and a strong sense of national pride among the Irish people. She is a potent reminder of the enduring power of mythology and its ability to shape cultural identity.

shortDescription: Ériu is an Irish goddess and one of the three sovereign goddesses of Ireland. She is the namesake of Ireland itself and embodies the land's power.
